content:
Let \(O\) be the center of the big circle \(C_2\) of radius \(9\), and let \(O_1\) be the center of the small circle \(C_1\) of (unknown) radius \(r\).  Since \(C_1\) is internally tangent to \(C_2\) at \(A\), we may place coordinates so that
\[
O=(0,0),\quad O_1=(9-r,0),\quad A=(9,0).
\]
Let \(P=(x_P,y_P)\) be the point on \(C_1\) whose tangent passes through \(O\).  The condition “the tangent at \(P\) to \(C_1\) goes through \(O\)” is equivalent to
\[
\overrightarrow{O_1P}\;\perp\;\overrightarrow{OP},
\]
so in triangle \(O\,O_1\,P\) the right angle is at \(P\).  Hence
\[
OO_1^2 \;=\; OP^2 \;+\; O_1P^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(9-r)^2 \;=\; OP^2 \;+\;r^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
OP^2 \;=\;81-18r.
\]
Thus
\[
OP=\sqrt{81-18r}\,.
\]

Next, the ray \(OP\) meets the big circle again at \(Q\), so \(OQ=9\) and
\[
PQ=OQ-OP=9-OP.
\]
The tangent to \(C_1\) at \(A\) is the vertical line \(x=9\).  It meets the line \(OP\) again at a point \(R\).  Parametrizing
\[
OP:\quad (x,y)=t\,(x_P,y_P),
\]
we hit \(x=9\) when \(t=9/x_P\), so
\[
R=\Bigl(9,\;9\frac{y_P}{x_P}\Bigr),
\]
and hence
\[
OR=\sqrt{9^2+\Bigl(9\frac{y_P}{x_P}\Bigr)^2}
=\frac{9}{x_P}\sqrt{x_P^2+y_P^2}
=\frac{9}{x_P}\,OP.
\]
Thus
\[
QR=OR-OQ=\frac{9}{x_P}OP-9.
\]
The condition \(PQ=QR\) becomes
\[
9-OP \;=\;\frac{9}{x_P}OP-9,
\]
or
\[
\frac{9\,OP}{x_P}=18-OP.
\]
One also checks (from the cyclic and perpendicularity conditions) that
\[
x_P=\frac{81-18r}{9-r},\qquad OP^2=81-18r,
\]
and a small algebraic check shows the only non–degenerate solution is
\[
r=4,\quad x_P=1.8,\quad OP=\sqrt{81-18\cdot4}=\sqrt9=3.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]

message.content:
Let \(O\) be the center of the big circle \(C_2\) of radius \(9\), and let \(O_1\) be the center of the small circle \(C_1\) of (unknown) radius \(r\).  Since \(C_1\) is internally tangent to \(C_2\) at \(A\), we may place coordinates so that
\[
O=(0,0),\quad O_1=(9-r,0),\quad A=(9,0).
\]
Let \(P=(x_P,y_P)\) be the point on \(C_1\) whose tangent passes through \(O\).  The condition “the tangent at \(P\) to \(C_1\) goes through \(O\)” is equivalent to
\[
\overrightarrow{O_1P}\;\perp\;\overrightarrow{OP},
\]
so in triangle \(O\,O_1\,P\) the right angle is at \(P\).  Hence
\[
OO_1^2 \;=\; OP^2 \;+\; O_1P^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(9-r)^2 \;=\; OP^2 \;+\;r^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
OP^2 \;=\;81-18r.
\]
Thus
\[
OP=\sqrt{81-18r}\,.
\]

Next, the ray \(OP\) meets the big circle again at \(Q\), so \(OQ=9\) and
\[
PQ=OQ-OP=9-OP.
\]
The tangent to \(C_1\) at \(A\) is the vertical line \(x=9\).  It meets the line \(OP\) again at a point \(R\).  Parametrizing
\[
OP:\quad (x,y)=t\,(x_P,y_P),
\]
we hit \(x=9\) when \(t=9/x_P\), so
\[
R=\Bigl(9,\;9\frac{y_P}{x_P}\Bigr),
\]
and hence
\[
OR=\sqrt{9^2+\Bigl(9\frac{y_P}{x_P}\Bigr)^2}
=\frac{9}{x_P}\sqrt{x_P^2+y_P^2}
=\frac{9}{x_P}\,OP.
\]
Thus
\[
QR=OR-OQ=\frac{9}{x_P}OP-9.
\]
The condition \(PQ=QR\) becomes
\[
9-OP \;=\;\frac{9}{x_P}OP-9,
\]
or
\[
\frac{9\,OP}{x_P}=18-OP.
\]
One also checks (from the cyclic and perpendicularity conditions) that
\[
x_P=\frac{81-18r}{9-r},\qquad OP^2=81-18r,
\]
and a small algebraic check shows the only non–degenerate solution is
\[
r=4,\quad x_P=1.8,\quad OP=\sqrt{81-18\cdot4}=\sqrt9=3.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
